---
author:
  - name: "Christina Muedsam"
date: last-modified
number-sections: false
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

<!-- Kept as an .unnumbered H1 rather than a YAML `title:` -- a YAML title makes
     Quarto count index.qmd as a numbered chapter, which shifts every
     subsequent chapter number by one. Quarto promotes this heading into
     #title-block-header, which the landing page hides in CSS (the hero below
     carries the title visually). -->

# Project Hail Mary {.unnumbered}

```{=html}
<!-- ===================================================================
     Landing hero: the reasoning graph IS the navigation. Nodes and edges
     are built by assets/network-nav.js from assets/hail-mary-data.js;
     everything below the fold is the written overview.
     Under 820px the graph is swapped for .hm-fallback-list (see the CSS).
     =================================================================== -->
<div class="hm-hero" data-hm-hero>

  <div class="hm-graph"></div>

  <header class="hm-header">
    <div class="hm-wordmark">
      <span class="hm-mark">POTYVIRUS</span>
      <span class="sub">· PROJECT HAIL MARY</span>
      <a href="about.html">ABOUT</a>
      <a href="data-code.html">DATA &amp; CODE</a>
    </div>
    <div class="hm-controls">
      <div class="hm-segmented" role="group" aria-label="Reading level">
        <button type="button" data-hm-audience="expert" aria-pressed="true">expert</button>
        <button type="button" data-hm-audience="plain" aria-pressed="false">plain</button>
      </div>
      <button type="button" class="hm-switch" data-hm-expand-all aria-pressed="false">
        <span class="track"><span class="knob"></span></span>
        EXPAND ALL
      </button>
    </div>
  </header>

  <div class="hm-title-card">
    <div class="hm-eyebrow">SEQUENCE · ECOLOGY · PROTEIN SORTING</div>
    <h1>Project Hail<br><span class="accent">Mary</span></h1>
    <p class="hm-tagline">a chain of <span class="accent">(deep)</span> thought</p>
    <p class="hm-standfirst">
      Following one 15-nucleotide coincidence all the way to a hypothesis about
      how a plant virus hijacks a cell's sorting machinery.
    </p>
    <p class="hm-explain">
      Each node is a chapter; each edge is a step in the reasoning —
      <em>what led to what</em>. Start at the spark, end at the synthesis.
    </p>
    <div class="hm-legend">
      <div class="item"><span class="dot spark"></span><span>the spark</span></div>
      <div class="item"><span class="dot gold"></span><span>the hypothesis</span></div>
    </div>
    <div class="hm-hint">
      click a node to expand<br>drag to rearrange · toggle to reveal all
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="hm-fallback-list" aria-label="Chapters"></div>
  <div class="hm-scroll-cue">scroll for the written overview</div>
</div>
```

*DNAzyme-like motifs, horizontal gene transfer, and short linear motif
conservation in Potyviridae* [⚑ preprint — not peer reviewed]{.preprint-badge}

::: {.column-margin}
**⚑ Preprint — not peer reviewed**

This collection is under active development and has not been peer reviewed.
Much of it is exploratory, and several of the central ideas (in particular the
DNAzyme-activity, horizontal-gene-transfer, and AP-4 / tyrosine-motif immune
hypotheses) are presented as explicit, testable hypotheses rather than
established results — they are flagged as such throughout. Analyses, figures,
interpretations and conclusions may change; please treat everything here as a
living draft and cite with that caveat. Feel free to contribute. Constructive feedback is always welcome (no matter who you are or what your background is!).
:::

### Explore by thread

The graph at the top of this page is the primary map: chapters as nodes, the
reasoning that connects them as edges. The table below covers the same ground
as a plain list.


The work spans several interconnected research threads:

| Chapter | Topic | Key tools |
|---|---|---|
| [1 — Discovery](notebooks/01_discovery.qmd) | DNAzyme match + eukaryotic homologs | BLAST, Biostrings |
| [2 — Phylogenetics](notebooks/02_phylogenetics.qmd) | CI protein tree, motif mapping | MAFFT, IQ-TREE 2, ape |
| [3 — Codon Usage](notebooks/03_codon_dnazyme.qmd) | RSCU, ENC, host adaptation, DNAzyme locus | potytools, seqinr |
| [4 — ELM Motifs](notebooks/04_protein_motifs.qmd) | Short linear motif conservation in CI | ELM REST API, potytools |
| [5 — Potyvirus Motifs](notebooks/05_potyvirus_motifs.qmd) | CI 3D structure, motif catalogue, interaction network | AlphaFold, igraph, visNetwork |
| [6 — HGT](notebooks/06_hgt.qmd) | Phylogenetic incongruence, sequence logos | ggseqlogo, potytools |
| [7 — Networks](notebooks/07_networks.qmd) | Host-virus ecological networks | igraph, BioNexus app |
| [8 — Immunity](notebooks/08_immu_simmu.qmd) | PTI/ETI simulation under genetic perturbations | Observable JS |
| [9 — AP-4 Trafficking](notebooks/09_ap4_trafficking.qmd) | AP-4/tyrosine-motif immune-trafficking hypothesis | — |


## Lessons learned

- A DNAzyme catalytic domain sequence matches perfectly to a region in the genomes of several potyviruses (6 species, 16 isolates).
- The flanking regions of those DNAzyme-matching sequences (16 isolates, 9 identical by sequence) are identical to regions in the genomes of several eukaryotes, including plants (potential viral hosts) and insects (potential viral vectors).
- Interactions between those eukaryotic species suggest the homology might reflect cross-kingdom horizontal DNA transfer.
- The eukaryotic genes span diverse functions — DNA repair, transcription regulation, chromatin remodelling, RNA processing, translation, and genes of unknown function — several of them essential for cell survival and development.
- The CI protein also carries conserved short linear motifs (ELM) beyond the DNAzyme-matching sequence itself, including an accessible tyrosine-based (YXXΦ) sorting signal alongside an acidic-dileucine AP-2 motif, recurring with genus-specific variation across isolates.
- Those sorting motifs motivate a testable hypothesis that the CI protein co-opts host intracellular trafficking — potentially via the AP-4 adaptor complex — to aid viral replication and spread, and to perturb the same trafficking machinery used in plant immune signalling. 
- The motif is conserved across human immune receptors, as well as human pathogens and might a) explain massive expansion of the genus Potyvirus (in comparisons to other Potyviridae) and b) represent the functional link between viral infections to delayed onset of health disorders like autoimmune diseases and cancer. 


### Reproducibility

All computationally intensive steps (alignment, phylogenetic inference, NCBI
fetching, ELM queries) are orchestrated by a `targets` pipeline:

```r
# Install the pipeline dependencies once
install.packages(c("targets", "tarchetypes"))
remotes::install_local("packages/potytools")

# Run the full pipeline (skips up-to-date steps automatically)
targets::tar_make()

# Visualise dependencies
targets::tar_visnetwork()
```

External tools required on `PATH` for running the entire pipeline: `mafft`, `iqtree2`, `hmmscan`.

## Interactive Companion App

The **BioNexus** Shiny app provides live GBIF + GLOBI biodiversity network
exploration relevant to Chapters 7 and 8:

```r
shiny::runApp("shiny/bionexus/")
```
<!-- 
## Publication Plans

This material is intended for at least two separate publications:

1. **Paper 1** — The DNAzyme discovery + HGT evidence (Chapters 1, 2, 6).
   Target: *PNAS* / *eLife* / *PLoS Pathogens*.
2. **Paper 2** — ELM short linear motif conservation in Potyviridae CI proteins
   (Chapters 3, 4, 5). Target: *Virus Evolution* / *Journal of Virology*.
3. **Paper 3** (optional) — Ecological host networks and immunity modelling
   (Chapters 7–9). Target: *New Phytologist* / *MPMI*. -->

## Package

Analysis functions are collected in the **potytools** R package
(`packages/potytools/`), being prepared for rOpenSci peer review.

## Project History

| Date | Milestone |
|---|---|
| Feb 2026 | DNAzyme match first identified (`2025_toys/poty.qmd`) |
| Mar 2026 | HGT analysis suite |
| Apr 2026 | Codon usage analysis iterations |
| May 2026 | Cleaned codon + DNAzyme notebook |
| May 2026 | Final phylogenetic pipeline |
| Jun 2026 | ELM motif & immunity notebooks; this document |



## Overview

This collection documents the discovery and characterisation of a DNAzyme-like
catalytic domain sequence that maps precisely to the genomes of multiple
Potyviridae species, with flanking regions matching eukaryotic genes across
plant, fungal, and animal kingdoms. Cross-kingdom horizontal gene transfer is a possible explanation and presence-absence analyses of the potentially viral fragment in related plant species support the idea. However, considering that activity-critical positions show substitutions in homologous Potyvirus species, DNAzyme activity due to the presence of the detected sequence, in vivo, and especially DNAzymatic activity of homologous Potyvirus sequences, is still unlikely or a very rare event - and would additionally require conciding reverse transcriptase activity in the host. 

Additionally, the Potyviridae DNAzyme-like sequence, corresponds to a conserved protein motif in the viral CI helicase. Motif homology and the CIs' subcellular distribution and regulation suggest that the tyrosine-based motif could be involved in viral-host interactions associated with intracellular sorting (potentially via AP-4), viral replication, viral spread and immunity. The motif is conserved across species and kingdoms and known to be involved in and crucial for processes such as autophagy and endosomal processing and correct targeting. 

### Long-shot hypothesis

I further suspect that i) defects in components orchestrating protein sorting, recycling and degradation, or ii) viral particle overload (e.g. during infections) could result in endosomal jamming, lipid distortions and sporadic reversal of membrane (incl. receptor or transporter) topologies due to insufficient coordination at the late endosome. 
I also suspect that this mechanism applies not only to the plant system. Motif and pathway conservation instead suggest that it might be conserved in multicellular organisms.

### The reasoning chain

The paragraphs above compress a lot of branching logic into prose. Laid out
as an actual argument tree instead, it's easier to see which parts are
empirical, which are well-supported inference, and which are still
explicitly open hypotheses rather than conclusions:

<div class="reasoning-map">
<div class="legend">
<div class="item"><span class="swatch established"></span><span class="label"><b>Established</b> — empirical, cited</span></div>
<div class="item"><span class="swatch supported"></span><span class="label"><b>Well-supported</b> inference</span></div>
<div class="item"><span class="swatch open"></span><span class="label"><b>Open</b> — explicit hypothesis, untested</span></div>
<div class="item"><span class="swatch deprioritized"></span><span class="label"><b>Deprioritized</b>, not rejected</span></div>
<div class="item"><span class="tag-next" style="position:static">→ next step</span><span class="label">concrete path to closing it</span></div>
</div>
<div class="tree">
<div class="node established root">
<div class="t">10–23nt DNAzyme catalytic core → BLAST match</div>
<div class="d">16 potyvirus isolates, 6 species — perfect match</div>
</div>
<div class="stem"></div>
<div class="branches">
<div class="branch">
<div class="label">Is it active?</div>
<div class="node deprioritized">
<div class="t">Activity-critical positions substituted</div>
<div class="d">unlikely / rare event</div>
</div>
<div class="sub">
<div class="node supported">
<div class="t">Codon-partitioned dN/dS: purifying selection at the protein level</div>
<div class="d">family-wide, 13 genera (Ch. 2)</div>
<div class="tag-next">→ GARD recombination screen not yet run</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="branch">
<div class="label">Is it HGT?</div>
<div class="node open">
<div class="t">Ecological network: sporadic, not continuous</div>
</div>
<div class="connector"></div>
<div class="node supported">
<div class="t">Strawberry insert: lineage-specific, fused to TCP7</div>
<div class="d">origin (HGT vs. else) still open</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="branch">
<div class="label">What is the locus?</div>
<div class="node supported">
<div class="t">Maps to CI helicase: YXXΦ + AP-2 sorting motif</div>
</div>
<div class="connector"></div>
<div class="node supported">
<div class="t">CI co-opts host trafficking via AP-4</div>
</div>
<div class="connector"></div>
<div class="node main">
<div class="t">MAIN BRANCH — AP-4 disruption → topology flip</div>
<div class="d">AP-4 ↔ autophagy · AP-4 ↔ BDA1 (Huang et al., plant autoimmunity)</div>
<div class="d" style="color:var(--flag)">→ autoimmune disease (Crohn's) · cancer?</div>
<div class="tag-next">→ reassess PhD NRAMP3/4 ap-4 mis-sorting data</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>



<!--
### Life-lessons learned

- it takes an afternoon to hand-check stuff and a month to automate a systematic approach
- it takes another month to make the output look nice and a lifetime to make it look nice to everyone
- annoying questions often turn out to be the most relevant
- - I think it was march when I had a look at the protein sequence for the first time. My PhD thesis must have turned me into a pattern recognition engine - YxxLMVI caught my eye. As before, knowledge gain a day later without any tool vs knowledge gain a couple of months later without any payment (nor a job, nor as many applications written as I should have and could have if I had not tried to make this look prettier and to allow someone to maybe get the whole point ... or not) at this point of my life seems like the biggest, most pointless and stupid way I ever found to waste time I dont even have.
- when you are trying to answer a difficult question, you solve at least 3 partially related but sometimes more relevant problems, but not unless you go out for a walk every now and then.
- biology is nice and being a biologist is also nice, but it does not reach awesomemess until you realize that noone can keep you from using the rest of your brain, too (anymore).
- Standing on the shoulders of giants with nothing but a 13inch screen and public data allows you to come up with a disruptive hypothesis within a couple of days, but to get funding for all the stuff you need to prove yourself wrong (in case you are wrong) requires you to climb down just to then creep up someone's butt. And whoever ends up testing your hypothesis will act as if you don't exist in case you were right unless you do it yourself, but then your results will be questioned.
- you don't rant about stuff you don't care about, but when you rant people assume you hate, even when you are passionate, instead.


### Side-quests and follow-ups

- if the presence of viral sequences in plants (or insects) indeed turns out to be originating from HGT, it's another evidence that many of such events are still remaining undetected. 
- DNAzyme activity might not occur on its own in biological systems, but did anyone ever try to turn it around and fight potyviruses with DNAzymes?! One of the limiting factors in medicine might not be limiting in plants (presence of magnesium-ions)
- one of the annoying but important questions was "ssDNA in a living cell? When is there ssDNA in a cell, at all?!". And the answer to that brings me to one of the few branches of the tree of life that are otherwise excluded in this document: bacteria. Specifically: Agrobacteria which transmit part of their plasmid DNA into plant cells as ssDNA. 
-->
